messages.content: random thing i've always wondered, why do we get hiccups? like what's the actual point of them

Ha, hiccups are one of those things that seem so pointless it's almost funny.

The honest answer is **we're not totally sure** they serve any useful purpose in adults. But there are a few interesting angles:

**The reflex itself** is pretty well understood - something irritates or stimulates your diaphragm (eating too fast, carbonation, sudden temperature changes, etc.) and it spasms. The "hic" sound is your vocal cords snapping shut right after.

**The evolutionary theory** that gets thrown around a lot is that it might be a leftover reflex from when our ancestors had gills - it resembles the motor pattern fish and tadpoles use to flush water over gills. Which is kind of wild to think about.

**Another idea** is that it might help babies. The reflex is really strong in fetuses and newborns, and some researchers think it helps them practice breathing muscles or clear air from the stomach while feeding.

But for adults? It really does seem like mostly just an annoying glitch with no clear benefit. Your body doing something that made sense millions of years ago and never fully dropped it.

The fact that we still don't have a definitive cure kind of reflects how little we understand it too - holding your breath, drinking water upside down... none of it is proven to reliably work 😄

Did you have a specific angle you were curious about?
my niece had them forever yesterday and it got me curious about the whole thing

Aw that's cute that it sparked the curiosity! Kids getting hiccups is so dramatic too, their whole little body jolts 😄

How long is "forever" in this case - like was it actually a concerningly long time or just the usual annoying 10-15 minutes that feels like forever when you're watching someone hiccup?

Because there's actually a real distinction medically - hiccups lasting more than 48 hours are considered "persistent" and doctors do take that seriously. But the vast majority of cases are just the normal short burst kind that resolve on their own.

How old is she? Younger kids seem to get them so much more frequently than adults, which kind of fits with that theory about it being more of an active reflex early in life.
i swear everyone's got their own folk cure too. my grandma always did the spoonful of sugar thing

The spoonful of sugar one is actually pretty interesting because there might be something to it! The idea is that swallowing repeatedly and the texture/sensation could help reset the vagus nerve which is involved in the reflex. So it's not pure superstition at least.

But yeah everyone's got their version 😄 the classics are like:

- holding your breath
- drinking water upside down (how does anyone actually do this successfully)
- someone scaring you
- breathing into a paper bag
- pulling your knees to your chest

And they all kind of work on similar logic - either disrupting your breathing pattern or increasing CO2 levels which seems to calm the diaphragm spasms.

The scare one is funny because it's so hit or miss and then the person who tried to scare you is way too proud of themselves when it works 😄

The honest truth is they probably all work sometimes and fail sometimes, and we just remember the wins. Classic confirmation bias situation - nobody's keeping track of the times grandma's sugar spoon did absolutely nothing.

Did your family have any other weird ones beyond the sugar? Some of the regional/family specific ones people come up with are genuinely creative
it's kinda funny how something so common is apparently still a bit of a mystery science-wise

Right?? It's one of those things that makes you realize how much we just take for granted that science has figured everything out. Like hiccups are so mundane and universal, every single person has had them, and we're still kind of like "yeah we're not totally sure"

It's actually a pretty long list of really ordinary things that are still not fully understood - yawning is another big one. We don't really know why yawning is contagious, or even fully why we yawn at all. The "tired/need oxygen" explanation turns out to be pretty oversimplified.

Or like why we need to sleep. We know SO much about what happens during sleep and what goes wrong without it, but the fundamental question of why our brains need that unconscious downtime is still genuinely debated.

There's something kind of humbling about it honestly. We can do incredibly complex things medically but some guy hiccuping in the corner is still a bit of a shrug situation 😄

I think people assume science is this complete picture and it's more like... a really detailed map with some surprisingly random blank spots in the middle of otherwise well mapped territory

Does that kind of stuff interest you generally or was this more just a random afternoon curiosity from watching your niece suffer through her hiccups 😄
